coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of carbon dots with blue-emitting for fluorescence determination of alizarin yellow R and temperature
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Dianlong
Qu, Wenshan
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a nontoxic, quick and one-step hydrothermal strategy was used to develop nitrogen and sulfur doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs). Sulfapyridine was selected as a sulfur source, and m-phenylenediamine was applied as a nitrogen source. In addition, the as-developed N,S-CDs indicated excitation-dependent feature, strong blue-emitting fluorescence, excellent photostability and anti-ion effect. Furthermore, the as-established N,S-CDs displayed an excellent linear characteristic for the determination of alizarin yellow R. The detection limit was 0.25 μM. Moreover, the as-developed N-CDs were successfully applied to determine alizarin yellow R in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nitrogen and sulfur
Fluorescence sensors
Alizarin yellow R

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully appreciated the financial support for this study by Shanxi Province Higher Education General Teaching Reform and Innovation Project (grant no. J20231050).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (A), size distribution (B) and FT-IR spectrum of N,S-CDs.
XPS spectrum of C1s (A), N1s (B), O1s (C) and S2p (D).
(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum (red line), fluorescence excitation (green line) and emission spectrum (balck line) N,S-CDs; (B) The influence of excitation wavelength on the emission wavelength.
(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of N,S-CDs in the absence and presence of different alizarin yellow R; (B) The linear fitting curve.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum (red line) of alizarin yellow R, fluorescence excitation (green line) and emission spectrum (balck line) N,S-CDs; (B) The fluorescence lifetime of N,S-CDs in the absence and presence of alizarin yellow R.
(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of N,S-CDs under different temperature; (B) The linear fitting curve; (C) The cyclic curve.
Illustration of the preparation process of N,S-CDs and its application in alizarin yellow R detection.
The alizarin yellow R detection in real samples.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of carbon dots with blue-emitting for fluorescence determination of alizarin yellow R and temperature

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Shanxi Datong University, Datong, Shanxi, 037000, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Shanxi Datong University, Datong, Shanxi, 037000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a nontoxic, quick and one-step hydrothermal strategy was used to develop nitrogen and sulfur doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs). Sulfapyridine was selected as a sulfur source, and m-phenylenediamine was applied as a nitrogen source. In addition, the as-developed N,S-CDs indicated excitation-dependent feature, strong blue-emitting fluorescence, excellent photostability and anti-ion effect. Furthermore, the as-established N,S-CDs displayed an excellent linear characteristic for the determination of alizarin yellow R. The detection limit was 0.25 μM. Moreover, the as-developed N-CDs were successfully applied to determine alizarin yellow R in real samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Alizarin yellow R, a type of azo dye, can be prepared through the diazo coupling reaction [1]. It has been widely applied in some fields, such as plastics, textile, leather, food, paints, pharmaceuticals and lacquers industries [2,3]. The excessive concentration of alizarin yellow R can cause some hazards, such as contact dermatitis, rhinitis, asthma and allergies [4–6]. Therefore, it is crucial to develop a sensitive and selective method for the detection of alizarin yellow R. So far, few methods are applied to measure alizarin yellow R.

Specially, fluorescence nanoprobe can be used for the measurement of cations, anions, and organic compounds [7–9]. In the past decade, carbon dots (CDs) are a new-type research focus in nanotechnology [10–13]. The CDs have attracted tremendous attention in some fields, such as drug delivery, drug testing, cell imaging, biosensing and catalysis [14–16]. These applications are attributed to their special performances including high specific surface area, excellent optical performance, electrical performance and photostability [17,18]. However, the reported literatures exhibit that the fluorescence quantum yield of CDs are relatively low, which is not conducive to widespread use in many fields. Apparently, the band gap and electronic local density of CDs can be regulated via doping other elements with the rapid development of nanotechnology, which can obviously improve their fluorescence quantum yield [19–24]. So far, some heteroatoms have been applied to adjust the fluorescence quantum yield and fluorescence intensity of CDs, such as fluoride, sulfur, nitrogen and phosphorus [25–28]. Simultaneously, the surface functional group modification is an important strategy to improve the measurement sensitivity and selectivity of CDs for the detection experiments [29,30]. The main functional groups include phenol hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino and sulfydryl [31–33]. In recent years, the hydrothermal strategy has attracted tremendous attention due to its facile preparation procedure.

Herein, an environmental-friendly, quick, simple and one-step hydrothermal strategy was carried out for the synthesis of N,S-CDs by using sulfapyridine as a sulfur source and m-phenylenediamine as a nitrogen source. Based on the related experimental data, the N,S-CDs suggested excellent photostability, high ionic strength tolerance, strong fluorescence intensity and good water solubility. The fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs could be sensitively and selectively quenched by alizarin yellow R. A facile, sensitive and quick platform for trace quantitative measurement of alizarin yellow R was established and successfully used for the sensing of alizarin yellow R in real samples, such as river water, glucose sample and normal saline samples. The experimental results exhibited that the N,S-CDs could be applied as fluorescence nanoprobes for alizarin yellow R sensing in aqueous solutions. Moreover, the as-prepared N,S-CDs were used for temperature sensing.

m-phenylenediamine (98 %), sulfapyridine (≥99 %), glucose (98 %), glutathione (98 %), vitamin B6 (≥98 %), catechin (≥97 %), bisphenol A (>99 %), kanamycin (98 %), histamine (96 %), vitamin B1 (98 %), tryptophan (99 %), sodium fluoride (98 %), sodium chloride (99.5 %), sodium bromide (99 %), sodium iodide (99.5 %), potassium chloride (99.5 %), zinc chloride (≥98 %), calcium chloride (≥99 %), magnesium chloride (99 %), aluminum chloride (99 %), nickel chloride (98 %), sodium carbonate (99.5 %), sodium sulfite (99 %), sodium acetate (99 %), sodium sulfate (99 %), sodium bicarbonate (≥99.8 %), sodium bisulfite (99.5 %) and alizarin yellow R (98 %) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. The Merck Milli-Q ultrapure water purification system was applied for the preparation of ultrapure water.

The Hitachi-7700 Transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Hitachi, Japan) was applied to investigate the morphology of N,S-CDs at a set voltage of 200 kV. The Nicolet 6700 Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy was used to carry out the FT-IR spectrum of N,S-CDs. The scan range of FT-IR spectrum was 4000–400 cm−1. The oxidized state of elements in N,S-CDs were discussed via using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ThermoFisher ESCALAB 250 Xi). The fluorescence spectra of the detection system were studied on the F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan) at the voltage of 400 V. The scan range and excitation/emission slits were 380–600 nm and 10 nm/10 nm, respectively. The conjugated structures were carried out by ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra on the UV-4500 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan).

The N,S-CDs were established though the hydrothermal reaction of m-phenylenediamine and sulfapyridine. The preparation process of N,S-CDs was displayed in Scheme 1 . The m-phenylenediamine of 0.1 mg and ascorbic acid of 0.1 mg were dissolved into the ultrapure water of 30 mL with stirring for 30 min at room temperature. Subsequently, the transparent solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave at 200 ℃ for 6 h. The solution was cooled to room temperature and then filtered through filter paper. Finally, the solution was dialyzed for 48 h via a dialysis bag (3000 Da).

Firstly, the N,S-CDs solution (10 μL, 5.0 mg/mL) was dropped into the disodium hydrogen phosphate and potassium dihydrogen phosphate buffer solution (2.0 mL, pH = 6.5). Then, different alizarin yellow R concentrations (10 μL) were added into the above solution with slow stirring for 2.0 min. Subsequently, the mixture was placed at room temperature for 1 min. At last, the fluorescence emission data between 380 nm and 600 nm were carried out under the excitation wavelength of 355 nm. Each sample was tested three times in parallel.

The river water, glucose and normal saline samples were used to study the feasibility of N,S-CDs in actual environment. The river water sample was obtained from campus. The glucose and normal saline samples were purchased from pharmacy. The spiked solutions of alizarin yellow R were added into the river water, glucose and normal saline samples. Then, the fluorescence data were obtained from fluorescence spectrometer to analyze the recovery rate of alizarin yellow R.

The TEM image of the N,S-CDs was suggested in Fig. 1 A. From Fig. 1A, the morphology of N,S-CDs exhibited uniform small ball shape and excellent dispersibility. The particle size distribution of N,S-CDs was 2.9–3.5 nm, the average diameter of N,S-CDs was 3.2 nm (Fig. 1B). The surface groups of N,S-CDs were analyzed by FT-IR. In Fig. 1C, a wide characteristic peak at 3439.2 cm−1 (range from 3756.3 cm−1 to 3012.5 cm−1) was attributed to the stretching vibration of –OH or –NH. The massive –OH or –NH was ascribed to excellent hydrophilicity of N,S-CDs [34,35]. The feature peaks at 2817.9 cm−1 and 1631.4 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibration of C-H and C = O bonds, respectively [36,37]. Specially, a characteristic peak at 1117.5 cm−1 was observed, which corresponded to the stretching vibration of C-N bond [38], indicating that the nitrogen was successfully doped into the CDs.

The element type and state of N,S-CDs were continuously carried out via XPS characterization. As suggested in Fig. S1, the XPS full spectra of as-developed N,S-CDs displayed four classic peaks at 162.7 eV, 284.8 eV, 398.2 eV and 531.6 eV, which were assigned to S2p, C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively [39]. In XPS single spectrum of C1s (Fig. 2 A), three characteristic peaks at 284.6, 285.6 and 288.0 eV were attributed to C = C/C-C, C-N and C = O bonds, respectively [40]. As exhibited in Fig. 2B, a characteristic peak of C-N-C bond was observed located at 398.2 eV [41,42]. The O1s single spectrum (Fig. 2C) suggested peaks representing the O-H bond (centered at 530.8 eV), C = O bond (centered at 531.7 eV) and C-O bond (centered at 535.4 eV) [43,44]. In XPS single spectrum of S2p (Fig. 2D), five characteristic peaks at 161.5 eV, 163.2 eV, 164.4 eV, 167.7 eV and 168.9 eV were attributed to S2p3/2, S2p1/2, C-SO2 -, C-SO3 - and C-SO4 -, respectively [40]. The above results suggested that some active functional groups were formed on the surface of N,S-CDs, such as hydroxyl, amino and carboxyl. The XPS results were consistent with the FT-IR results. Based on the elemental analysis from XPS, the approximate contents of S, C, N and O were 8.04 %, 49.57 %%, 8.53 % and 33.87 %, respectively. The results further indicated that the nitrogen and sulfur were doped into the N,S-CDs.

The fluorescence and UV–vis absorption data of N,S-CDs were discussed through fluorescence and UV–vis spectrophotometer, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3 A, two feature absorption peaks at 289 nm and 328 nm were suggested in the UV–vis spectrum (red line) of N,S-CDs, which were attributed to the π → π⁎ transition of C = C bond and n → π⁎ transition of C = O bond. The fluorescence spectra (Fig. 3A) suggested that the emission wavelength was 441 nm (blank line) at the excitation wavelength of 355 nm (green line). To discuss the excitation-dependent or excitation-independent feature of N,S-CDs, the corresponding data were carried out via fluorescence spectrophotometer. As indicated in Fig. 3B, when the excitation wavelength was set as 340, 345, 350, 355, 360, 365 and 370 nm, the corresponding emission wavelength had no fixed value, suggesting the excitation-dependent performance of N,S-CDs. The fluorescence quantum yield was 17.3 %. The stability of the N,S-CDs was a key factor for the fluorescence detection. After the ultraviolet radiation of 5 min under UV lamp, the fluorescence quenching rate was only 5.85 %, suggesting the fascinating photostability of the N,S-CDs (Fig. S2A). The weakening phenomenon could be also observed in reported literatures [45]. As exhibited in Fig. S2B, when the sodium chloride concentration reached 0.05 M in the N,S-CDs solution, no weakening phenomenon of fluorescence intensity was observed, indicating the excellent salt resistance of N,S-CDs. The above results suggested that N,S-CDs had excellent application prospect in real samples.

Alizarin yellow R is one of the most important indicators in practical applications. Herein, we discussed the practicability of N,S-CDs as fluorescence nanosensors for the measurement of alizarin yellow R. The behavior of as-developed sensing platform was discussed via selecting the determination conditions, such as pH value and reaction time. Additionally, the difference of fluorescence intensity (ΔF = F0/F, where F0 and F stand for the fluorescence intensity of the N,S-CDs-based solution without and with alizarin yellow R) was obtained under different pH value and reaction time. As displayed in Fig. S3A, the influence of pH value (6, 6.5, 7, 7.5 and 8) on the determination platform for alizarin yellow R was studied. When the pH value was 6.5, the ΔF value reached maximum. Thus, 6.5 was selected as the determination condition. Meanwhile, the influence of reaction time on the as-developed measurement system were also carried out under different reaction time. As we can see from Fig. S3B, after the addition of alizarin yellow R, the ΔF value quickly achieved maximum, suggesting high sensitivity of N,S-CDs for alizarin yellow R. The above results implied the feasibility of rapid and sensitive measurement of alizarin yellow R in real samples.

The fluorescence spectra of the N,S-CDs-based solution for alizarin yellow R detection were displayed in Fig. 4 A. The peak height of emission spectra at 441 nm gradually decreased with alizarin yellow R amounts increasing from 0 to 100 μM at the excitation wavelength of 355 nm. The fluorescence data at 441 nm were used to discuss the linear relationship. As exhibited in Fig. 4B, a fascinating linear relationship between ln(F0/F) and alizarin yellow R amounts was developed at range of 0.5–100 μM. The linear equation was ln(F0/F) = 0.022[Q] + 0.104 (Q refers to the concentration of alizarin yellow R) with regression coefficient of 0.9979. In the light of LOD = 3σ/k, the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.25 μM.

Due to the presence of some interfering substances in real samples, the selectivity studying of probes is of great significance. The interfering substances included glucose, glutathione, vitamin B6, catechin, bisphenol A, kanamycin, histamine, vitamin B1, tryptophan, F-, Cl-, Br-, I-, Na+, K+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Ni2+, CO3 2–, SO3 2-,CH3COO–, SO4 2-, HCO3 – and HSO3 -. As displayed in Fig. S4, the amount of each interfering substance was 100 μM in the detection experiment. The results exhibited that alizarin yellow R possessed evident fluorescence weakening influence on the N,S-CDs, indicating that the N,S-CDs were highly selective for alizarin yellow R detection.

Moreover, the fluorescence quenching mechanisms were carried out via a series of experiments. As indicated in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence excitation spectrum of N,S-CDs overlapped the UV–vis absorption spectrum of alizarin yellow R, implying that the fluorescence quenching might be assigned to the inner filter effect [46,47]. The fluorescence quenching mechanism of as-established sensing platform was discussed further via recording the fluorescence lifetime of the N,S-CDs solution and N,S-CDs + alizarin yellow R mixture [48]. As we can see from Fig. 5B, the fluorescence lifetimes of N,S-CDs solution, N,S-CDs + alizarin yellow R mixture were 4.33 ns and 4.27 ns, respectively. During the inner filter effect procedure, the fluorescence lifetime of platform did not vary with increasing alizarin yellow R concentration [49]. Then, the inner filter effect should be observed during the detection process of alizarin yellow R.

To check the practicability of actual application, this developed nanoprobe was used for the measurement of alizarin yellow R in real samples. The pretreatment processes were displayed in section 2.4. We used the linear equation (ln(F0/F) = 0.022[Q] + 0.104) to discuss the relevant experimental data. The results were exhibited in Table 1 , the measured amounts were consistent with the added amounts of alizarin yellow R. Furthermore, the excellent recovery ratio of 95.8 %-105.2 % was obtained, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) of all samples were less than 4.96 %, suggesting the excellent accuracy and high dependability of the as-prepared nanosensor for measuring alizarin yellow R in river water, glucose and normal saline samples.

The peak height of emission spectra at 441 nm gradually decreased with temperature increasing from 15 to 95 ℃ at the excitation wavelength of 355 nm (Fig. 6 A). The fluorescence data were used to discuss the linear relationship. As exhibited in Fig. 6B, a fascinating linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and temperature with range of 15–95 ℃ was developed. The linear equation was F = -16.37[Tem] + 4164 with regression coefficient of 0.9686. The N,S-CDs exhibited good cycling performance for temperature sensing (Fig. 6C).

In conclusion, the nitrogen and sulfur doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) were prepared via a nontoxic, rapid and one-step hydrothermal strategy. Herein, the novelty was listed below (a) m-phenylenediamine and sulfapyridine were applied for the preparation of N,S-CDs with blue fluorescence; (b) the N,S-CDs with attenuated fluorescence characteristics were developed as fluorescence nanosensors for the sensitive and selective sensing of alizarin yellow R. Some instruments were used to discuss the composition, morphology, functional groups and optical properties of N,S-CDs. Additionally, the fluorescence weakening mechanism of the as-developed N,S-CDs for alizarin yellow R sensing was discussed. In the end, satisfactory recovery rate and RSD were acquired in real samples, indicating the excellent practicability of N,S-CDs. Our researches provided a new idea for facile and fast development of co-doped CDs and increased the detection application of N,S-CDs in real samples.
Dianlong Zhang: Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Wenshan Qu: Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully appreciated the financial support for this study by Shanxi Province Higher Education General Teaching Reform and Innovation Project (grant no. J20231050).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cplett.2024.141243.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
